certification.name: CompTIA Security+
certification.description: Vendor-neutral baseline cybersecurity certification across concepts, threats, architecture, operations, and governance.

objectives.title: Compare and contrast various types of security controls

concepts.title: Security control categories
concepts.description: Technical, managerial, operational, and physical controls — classified by HOW the control is implemented.

concepts.title: Preventive vs detective vs corrective controls
concepts.description: Preventive stops an incident before it happens; detective identifies one in progress or after; corrective limits damage once found. Deterrent, compensating, and directive controls fill related gaps.

objectives.title: Summarize fundamental security concepts

concepts.title: CIA triad
concepts.description: Confidentiality, Integrity, Availability — the three properties most security controls protect.

concepts.title: Non-repudiation
concepts.description: A party cannot credibly deny having performed an action, typically proven via digital signatures.

concepts.title: AAA (Authentication, Authorization, Accounting)
concepts.description: Proving identity, granting permissions, and logging activity — three distinct, sequential steps.

concepts.title: Authentication
concepts.description: Proving identity — the first step of AAA, distinct from authorization.

concepts.title: Zero Trust control plane vs data plane
concepts.description: The control plane (policy engine, policy administrator) decides access policy; the data plane (policy enforcement point) enforces it against real traffic.

concepts.title: Honeypot, honeynet, honeyfile, honeytoken
concepts.description: Decoy systems, networks, files, and data planted to detect and study attackers rather than to serve real users.

objectives.title: Explain the importance of change management processes and the impact to security

concepts.title: Change management process
concepts.description: Approval, impact analysis, testing, and a backout plan before a production change — security's stake in change control.

concepts.title: Allow list vs deny list
concepts.description: An allow list permits only explicitly approved items and blocks everything else (default-deny); a deny list blocks only named items and allows everything else (default-allow).

objectives.title: Explain the importance of using appropriate cryptographic solutions

concepts.title: PKI
concepts.description: The infrastructure of certificate authorities, certificates, and trust relationships binding public keys to identities.

concepts.title: Symmetric encryption
concepts.description: Same key encrypts and decrypts — fast, but the key must be shared securely.

concepts.title: Asymmetric encryption
concepts.description: A public/private key pair — what one key encrypts, only the other can decrypt.

concepts.title: TPM vs HSM
concepts.description: A TPM is a chip soldered to one device's motherboard for that device's own keys; an HSM is a dedicated, often external/networked appliance managing keys for many systems.

concepts.title: Tokenization vs masking
concepts.description: Tokenization swaps real data for a reversible reference token held in a separate vault; masking permanently obscures part of the data in place (e.g. showing only the last 4 digits).

concepts.title: Hashing
concepts.description: A one-way function producing a fixed-size fingerprint of data — verifies integrity, does not encrypt.

concepts.title: Salting
concepts.description: Adding random data to an input before hashing so identical inputs (e.g. two equal passwords) don't produce identical hashes.

concepts.title: Key stretching
concepts.description: Deliberately slowing a hash function (e.g. PBKDF2) with repeated iterations so brute-force password guessing becomes impractical.

concepts.title: Digital signatures
concepts.description: Signing a hash of a message with the sender's private key — proves authenticity, integrity, and non-repudiation together.

concepts.title: Certificates
concepts.description: A CA-signed document binding a public key to an identity.

concepts.title: CRL vs OCSP
concepts.description: A CRL is a downloaded list of all revoked certificates; OCSP is a live, single-certificate revocation check against the CA — OCSP scales better and is more current.

concepts.title: Wildcard certificate
concepts.description: A single certificate (e.g. *.example.com) valid for any subdomain of the named domain.

objectives.title: Compare and contrast common threat actors and motivations

concepts.title: Threat vs vulnerability vs risk
concepts.description: A threat is a potential danger; a vulnerability is a weakness it could exploit; risk is the likelihood and impact if it does.

concepts.title: Threat actor types and motivations
concepts.description: Nation-state (well-resourced, espionage), organized crime (financial gain), hacktivist (ideology), insider threat (internal access), unskilled attacker (low sophistication) — distinguished by resources and motive, not just method.

objectives.title: Explain common threat vectors and attack surfaces

concepts.title: Threat vector vs attack surface
concepts.description: A threat vector is the path an attack travels in (email, removable media, an open port); the attack surface is everything exposed that could be targeted.

concepts.title: Phishing vs vishing vs smishing
concepts.description: Same manipulation goal, different channel: phishing over email, vishing over voice calls, smishing over SMS text.

objectives.title: Explain various types of vulnerabilities

concepts.title: Zero-day vulnerability
concepts.description: A flaw unknown to the vendor with no patch available — exploited before a fix can exist.

concepts.title: VM escape
concepts.description: Breaking out of an isolated virtual machine to access the host or other VMs — the core risk virtualization is supposed to prevent.

concepts.title: SQL injection vs cross-site scripting
concepts.description: SQLi injects malicious database queries through unvalidated input; XSS injects malicious scripts that run in another user's browser. Both stem from trusting unvalidated input, but attack different targets.

objectives.title: Given a scenario, analyze indicators of malicious activity

concepts.title: Trojan vs worm vs virus
concepts.description: A virus needs a host file and user action to spread; a worm self-propagates across a network with no user action; a trojan disguises itself as legitimate software and doesn't self-replicate.

concepts.title: Rootkit vs logic bomb
concepts.description: A rootkit hides ongoing privileged access; a logic bomb sits dormant until a trigger condition executes a payload.

concepts.title: Amplified vs reflected DDoS
concepts.description: Reflected DDoS spoofs the victim's source IP so replies flood the victim; amplified DDoS additionally uses requests whose replies are much larger than the request, multiplying the flood.

concepts.title: Password spraying vs brute force
concepts.description: Brute force tries many passwords against one account; spraying tries one or few common passwords against many accounts to avoid lockout thresholds.

concepts.title: Indicators of malicious activity
concepts.description: Account lockout, impossible travel, and abnormal resource consumption are signs an account or system may be compromised, even before a clear alert fires.

objectives.title: Explain the purpose of mitigation techniques used to secure the enterprise

concepts.title: Network segmentation
concepts.description: Dividing a network into isolated zones so a breach in one zone can't freely reach another.

concepts.title: Least privilege
concepts.description: Granting only the minimum access needed to perform a job, nothing more.

concepts.title: Application allow listing
concepts.description: Only explicitly approved applications may run — everything else is blocked by default, applying the allow-list model to executables.

objectives.title: Compare and contrast security implications of different architecture models

concepts.title: Cloud shared responsibility matrix
concepts.description: The provider secures the cloud infrastructure itself; the customer remains responsible for securing what they put in it — the split shifts by service model (IaaS/PaaS/SaaS).

concepts.title: Virtualization vs containerization
concepts.description: Virtualization runs full guest OS instances on a hypervisor; containers share one host OS kernel and isolate only the application layer — lighter weight, less isolation.

concepts.title: Infrastructure as code
concepts.description: Defining and provisioning infrastructure through versioned configuration files instead of manual setup.

concepts.title: ICS/SCADA
concepts.description: Industrial control systems and the supervisory software that monitors them — typically prioritize uptime over patching, unlike IT systems.

objectives.title: Given a scenario, apply security principles to secure enterprise infrastructure

concepts.title: IDS vs IPS
concepts.description: An IDS passively monitors and alerts on suspicious traffic (detection); an IPS sits inline and can actively block it (prevention).

concepts.title: Jump server vs proxy server
concepts.description: A jump server is a hardened bastion host administrators pass through to reach a protected network; a proxy server relays and filters client requests to external resources.

concepts.title: Fail-open vs fail-closed
concepts.description: On failure, fail-open lets traffic pass unfiltered (favors availability); fail-closed blocks all traffic (favors security).

concepts.title: WAF vs NGFW vs UTM
concepts.description: A WAF inspects web application traffic specifically; an NGFW adds application-awareness to traditional firewalling; a UTM bundles many security functions (firewall, IPS, antivirus) into one appliance.

concepts.title: TLS vs IPSec tunneling
concepts.description: Both encrypt traffic in transit; TLS typically secures a specific application session, while IPSec operates at the network layer and can secure all traffic between two endpoints (site-to-site VPN).

objectives.title: Compare and contrast concepts and strategies to protect data

concepts.title: Data classification levels
concepts.description: Labeling data (public, private, confidential, restricted) so handling and protection requirements scale with sensitivity.

concepts.title: Data at rest vs in transit vs in use
concepts.description: Data at rest is stored; in transit is moving across a network; in use is actively processed in memory — each state needs different protections.

concepts.title: Data protection method selection
concepts.description: Choosing encryption, hashing, masking, tokenization, or segmentation based on whether data must remain usable, verifiable, or fully hidden.

objectives.title: Explain the importance of resilience and recovery in security architecture

concepts.title: Hot site vs warm site vs cold site
concepts.description: A hot site is fully equipped and can take over almost immediately; a warm site has some infrastructure ready but needs setup; a cold site is bare space requiring full buildout.

concepts.title: Load balancing vs clustering
concepts.description: Load balancing distributes traffic across independent servers; clustering groups servers to act as one highly-available unit, often with automatic failover.

concepts.title: Backup strategy fundamentals
concepts.description: Onsite/offsite placement, frequency, and encryption together determine how much data could be lost and how fast it can be restored.

objectives.title: Given a scenario, apply common security techniques to computing resources

concepts.title: Secure baseline
concepts.description: A documented, hardened starting configuration that systems are established from, deployed against, and continually maintained to match.

concepts.title: BYOD vs COPE vs CYOD
concepts.description: BYOD: employee owns and enrolls a personal device. COPE: company owns it, employee may use it personally. CYOD: employee picks from a company-approved device list; company owns it.

concepts.title: Mobile device management (MDM)
concepts.description: Centralized tooling to enforce policy, push configuration, and remotely wipe managed mobile devices.

concepts.title: WPA3
concepts.description: The current Wi-Fi security standard, replacing WPA2, with stronger encryption and protection against offline password-guessing attacks.

objectives.title: Explain the security implications of proper hardware, software, and data asset management

concepts.title: Asset lifecycle management
concepts.description: Tracking a device or license from acquisition through assignment, monitoring, and eventual decommissioning.

concepts.title: Sanitization vs destruction
concepts.description: Sanitization (e.g. wiping) removes data so the media can be reused; destruction physically or logically destroys the media so it cannot be reused at all.

objectives.title: Explain various activities associated with vulnerability management

concepts.title: Vulnerability scan vs penetration test
concepts.description: A vulnerability scan is automated and passive, listing potential weaknesses; a penetration test actively attempts to exploit them to prove real-world impact.

concepts.title: False positive vs false negative
concepts.description: A false positive flags something safe as a threat; a false negative misses a real threat entirely — false negatives are generally more dangerous.

concepts.title: CVSS vs CVE
concepts.description: A CVE is a unique identifier for a specific known vulnerability; CVSS is the standardized 0-10 score rating that vulnerability's severity.

concepts.title: Vulnerability remediation strategies
concepts.description: Patching fixes the flaw directly; compensating controls, segmentation, or accepted exceptions manage the risk when patching isn't immediately possible.

objectives.title: Explain security alerting and monitoring concepts and tools

concepts.title: SIEM
concepts.description: Aggregates logs from many sources into one system for correlated alerting, search, and reporting.

concepts.title: Log aggregation and alert tuning
concepts.description: Centralizing logs is only useful if alert thresholds are tuned — too sensitive causes alert fatigue, too loose misses real incidents.

concepts.title: Data loss prevention (DLP)
concepts.description: Tooling that detects and blocks sensitive data from leaving the organization through unauthorized channels.

objectives.title: Given a scenario, modify enterprise capabilities to enhance security

concepts.title: DMARC, DKIM, and SPF
concepts.description: Three complementary email-authentication standards: SPF checks the sending server is authorized, DKIM verifies the message wasn't altered in transit, DMARC tells receivers what to do when SPF/DKIM fail.

concepts.title: Network access control (NAC)
concepts.description: Evaluates a device's posture (patched, has antivirus, etc.) before allowing it onto the network.

concepts.title: EDR vs XDR
concepts.description: EDR monitors and responds to threats on individual endpoints; XDR correlates signals across endpoints, network, and other sources for broader detection.

objectives.title: Given a scenario, implement and maintain identity and access management

concepts.title: Single sign-on (SSO)
concepts.description: One authentication grants access to multiple independent systems without re-entering credentials.

concepts.title: Federation
concepts.description: Extending trust in an identity across separate organizations, so one org's login works at another's service.

concepts.title: SAML
concepts.description: An XML-based standard for exchanging SSO authentication and authorization assertions, common in enterprise identity federation.

concepts.title: OAuth
concepts.description: An authorization framework letting an app access another service's resources on a user's behalf, without handling the user's password.

concepts.title: OpenID Connect
concepts.description: An authentication layer built on top of OAuth 2.0 — OAuth authorizes access to resources, OIDC additionally verifies who the user is.

concepts.title: RBAC vs DAC vs MAC
concepts.description: Role-based grants access by job role; discretionary lets the data owner decide who gets access; mandatory enforces a central authority's classification-based policy that owners cannot override.

concepts.title: Multi-factor authentication
concepts.description: Combining two or more distinct factor categories — something you know, have, are, or somewhere you are — so a single stolen credential isn't enough.

concepts.title: Privileged access management (PAM)
concepts.description: Just-in-time elevation, password vaulting, and ephemeral credentials that grant privileged access only when needed and for a limited time.

concepts.title: Password best practices
concepts.description: Length matters more than forced complexity; password managers and passwordless methods reduce reuse risk better than frequent forced rotation.

objectives.title: Explain the importance of automation and orchestration related to secure operations

concepts.title: Automation benefits vs risks
concepts.description: Automation saves time and enforces consistent baselines, but concentrates risk — a scripting error or single point of failure can now scale instantly across the whole environment.

objectives.title: Explain appropriate incident response activities

concepts.title: Incident response process
concepts.description: The ordered phases: preparation, detection, analysis, containment, eradication, recovery, and lessons learned.

concepts.title: Containment vs eradication
concepts.description: Containment stops an incident from spreading further right now; eradication removes the root cause afterward so it can't recur.

concepts.title: Chain of custody
concepts.description: An unbroken, documented record of who handled evidence and when — without it, evidence may be inadmissible or untrustworthy.

objectives.title: Given a scenario, use data sources to support an investigation

concepts.title: Log data sources for investigation
concepts.description: Firewall, endpoint, application, and network logs each capture a different vantage point — a full investigation usually needs several correlated together.

concepts.title: Event vs alert vs incident
concepts.description: An event is any logged occurrence; an alert is an event flagged as potentially significant; an incident is a confirmed, actionable security event requiring response.

objectives.title: Summarize elements of effective security governance

concepts.title: Security policy types
concepts.description: AUP, incident response, business continuity, and SDLC policies each govern a distinct area — a policy states intent, a standard states the specific required configuration.

concepts.title: BCP vs DR
concepts.description: Business continuity planning keeps critical operations running during a disruption; disaster recovery restores systems and data afterward.

concepts.title: Data owner vs controller vs processor vs custodian
concepts.description: The owner is accountable for the data's classification and use; the controller decides how/why it's processed; the processor handles it on the controller's behalf; the custodian handles day-to-day technical safeguarding.

objectives.title: Explain elements of the risk management process

concepts.title: Qualitative vs quantitative risk analysis
concepts.description: Qualitative ranks risk using descriptive categories (low/medium/high); quantitative assigns real dollar figures to likelihood and impact.

concepts.title: SLE, ALE, and ARO
concepts.description: SLE = expected loss from one occurrence; ARO = how many times per year it's expected to happen; ALE = SLE × ARO, the expected annual loss.

concepts.title: Risk transfer vs accept vs avoid vs mitigate
concepts.description: Transfer shifts risk to another party (e.g. insurance); accept knowingly keeps it; avoid eliminates the risky activity entirely; mitigate reduces likelihood or impact with controls.

concepts.title: RTO vs RPO
concepts.description: RTO is the maximum acceptable time to restore a service after disruption; RPO is the maximum acceptable amount of data loss, measured in time since the last good backup.

objectives.title: Explain the processes associated with third-party risk assessment and management

concepts.title: Vendor risk assessment
concepts.description: Evaluating a third party's security posture — via penetration test results, audit evidence, or a right-to-audit clause — before and during a business relationship.

concepts.title: SLA vs MOU vs NDA vs MSA
concepts.description: An SLA sets measurable service commitments; an MOU documents mutual intent without binding legal obligations; an NDA protects confidential information; an MSA sets the overarching terms governing future work orders.

objectives.title: Summarize elements of effective security compliance

concepts.title: Compliance monitoring
concepts.description: Ongoing due diligence, attestation, and automation that confirm controls remain in place — not just a one-time audit checkbox.

concepts.title: Right to be forgotten
concepts.description: A privacy-regulation right (e.g. under GDPR) allowing an individual to request their personal data be deleted.

objectives.title: Explain types and purposes of audits and assessments

concepts.title: Internal vs external audit
concepts.description: Internal audits are performed by the organization's own staff for self-assessment; external audits are performed by an independent third party, often for regulatory or contractual assurance.

concepts.title: Known vs partially known vs unknown environment testing
concepts.description: Known environment (white box): tester has full information. Partially known (gray box): tester has some. Unknown (black box): tester starts with none, simulating a real external attacker.

objectives.title: Given a scenario, implement security awareness practices

concepts.title: Security awareness training
concepts.description: Ongoing phishing simulations and user guidance that build a workforce's ability to recognize and report social engineering, not just a one-time onboarding session.

concepts.title: Insider threat recognition
concepts.description: Recognizing risky, unexpected, or unintentional user behavior as a potential indicator of insider threat, distinct from external attack indicators.
